summary:
Tell Qaramel is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic site located near Aleppo in northern Syria, with occupation dating to approximately 9500–7500 BCE. The site is notable for preserving several circular stone towers that predate similar structures at Jericho and may represent some of the earliest known monumental construction in the ancient world. These towers, along with a range of architectural remains, demonstrate that early Neolithic communities in northern Syria were engaged in organized communal labor and substantial settlement planning.

The material culture of Tell Qaramel includes a rich assemblage of flint tools, bone implements, and early evidence for interaction with neighboring regions across the northern Fertile Crescent. The site lies in a zone where early Neolithic innovations — architecture, agriculture, animal husbandry — were developing simultaneously, and it contributes to the emerging picture of the northern Levant and upper Euphrates basin as a central region in the emergence of complex sedentary life.

images.caption: Vue aérienne du site archéologique de Tell Qaramel près d'Alep en Syrie
